chunk_summary: This section focuses on dietary and feeding assessments tailored to children with medical risks or atypical development, emphasizing condition-specific modifications to ensure safety, adequate intake, and caregiver support.
learning_objectives: Understand feeding readiness and growth monitoring in preterm infants and NICU graduates.
Identify condition-specific dietary modifications for chronic diseases like CHD, CKD, and CF.
Evaluate sensory-driven feeding challenges in neurodevelopmental conditions such as ASD.
Develop strategies to reduce caregiver burden while maintaining feeding safety and nutritional adequacy.
key_facts: Preterm infants require feeding readiness assessment by corrected age and close monitoring of growth velocity post-discharge.
Children with CHD and chronic lung disease have higher energy needs, necessitating smaller, frequent meals.
CKD dietary assessments must account for electrolyte restrictions, including high-potassium and high-phosphate foods.
Cystic fibrosis dietary evaluations should include enzyme adherence and fat intake patterns.
Neurodevelopmental conditions like ASD often involve sensory-driven food aversions, requiring documentation of triggers and family coping strategies.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: In children with neuromotor impairment (including CP), prioritize swallow safety and endurance; small improvements in posture, pacing, and texture can markedly change intake. (NICE guideline NG62)
document_key_topics: Feeding readiness in preterm infants and NICU graduates
Condition-specific dietary modifications for chronic diseases
Sensory-driven feeding challenges in neurodevelopmental disorders
Caregiver burden and practical feeding strategies
